Intense situations, the traditional negotiating advice is to keep a poker face and not get emotional. Up until recently, most theory misunderstood the role of emotion in negotiations. Emotions were considered obstacles that need to be overcome. The thinking was that you need to separate people from the problems. But I want you to think about that for a moment. If people are the problems, how can you separate people from the problem? The importance of emotion in any negotiation is vastly misunderstood. As it turns out, it's not emotions that are the problem, it's negative emotions. People who think emotions are bad for negotiations would tell you that water is bad for frogs, because boiling water would kill a frog. It's not water, it's boiling water. And as a matter of fact, frogs go faster in the right temperature of water. And just like the right temperature of water, you actually need positive emotions to think better. We're about 31% more capable mentally in a positive frame of mind. In a positive mindset, pattern recognition is quicker, decision making is more sound and less fear-based. Positive emotions actually enhance our thinking. It's also about how we make decisions. We make decisions based on what we care about. Care equals emotions. Negative emotions slow down our thinking. And anger gets us thinking along the wrong track. When people get upset with one another, understanding flies out the window. And that's when communication gets derailed. There's an old saying, give a speech when you're angry, and it will be the greatest speech you will ever regret. What we're talking about here, and we'll talk about it throughout the course, is what we call tactical empathy. We're going to get tactical in our application of empathy. We just know too much about how the brain works these days as a result of neuroscience to not be able to anticipate what we're looking for. This is next level empathy. Weapons, grade, empathy, if you will. In general, empathy has two components. Recognition and articulation. The ability to recognize the perspective of a counterpart, and then vocalize that recognition to articulate it, to state it, to verbally observe it. How we're going to define it is very close to what Daniel Goldman calls cognitive empathy in his book Focus. It doesn't require you to identify with someone's emotions, to feel them, to share them, to agree with them, or disagree with them. You're only identifying them and then articulating them. It's a very neutral position that frees you up to use it with anyone. Its application then becomes limitless and it's insanely effective at building trust-based influence, the most lasting and fear-resistant type of influence. It's important to understand what tactical empathy is not. I didn't say anything about agreeing or disagreeing with the other person's values or beliefs. It's not giving out hugs. That would be sympathy. Bob Nukin of the Harvard Program on Negotiation says in his brilliant book Beyond Winning, that empathy is not about being nice. So what is empathy then? What is this thing called empathy? And let's get more precise, tactical empathy. It's understanding the feelings and mindset of another, and also hearing what's behind those feelings. So you can increase your influence at all the moments that follow. Tactical empathy helps us learn the position the counterpart is in, why their actions make sense to them, and what might move them. The tactical application of empathy, the advanced application of it, is knowing that we are looking for both the negative emotions and the positive emotions, and that each have a different degree of impact. Negative emotions have roughly three times the impact of positive emotions, three times or more. Negatives create three times or more the size of the obstacles. As a result, the reasons for not doing something are more impactful than the reasons for doing something. Consequently, we're more concerned with negatives, and we don't ignore them and hope they go away. We also know that to deny them is the wrong way to address them, and is the only approach that will make them worse. The positive emotions often represent the pathways to agreement. It's just that the negatives create such significantly bigger barriers that you save a tremendous amount of time by clearing the barriers first. Tactical empathy is also knowing that every negative has a flipside positive, the other side of the coin, if you will. We can then begin to make strategic or tactical choices as to how we diffuse, reverse from, or enhance emotions. As I like to say, it's emotional intelligence on steroids. In order to become a great negotiator, I'm asking you to avoid entering the making an argument approach to negotiations, which usually doesn't persuade anyone of anything anyway. There's an old saying, when the argument lose the relationship. To achieve your goals, first put them aside to see things from your counterparts' perspective. It's the Stephen Covey guidance. Seek first to understand, then be understood. Don't treat emotions as obstacles. Treat them as tools that are means by which you resolve the negotiation. This will increase your emotional intelligence. It means opening up your senses, talking less, and listening more. You can learn almost anything you need, and a lot more than people would like you to know, simply by watching and listening, keeping your eyes peeled, your ears open, and most importantly, your mouth shut. Good negotiators don't deny emotions. They identify and use them to influence their counterpart. Those are the skills you will learn in this course.
